draft.introduction: 1 Samuel 3 recounts the call of the boy Samuel, who hears God’s voice in the night and initially mistakes it for his mentor Eli’s. The passage is a paradigm of prophetic vocation: God calls, and the human learns to respond. From the Septuagint.

translation.text: And the Lord called, “Samuel! Samuel!” And he said, “Here I am!” And he ran to Eli, and said, “Here I am, for you called me.” But he said, “I did not call; go back and lie down.” And the Lord called again, “Samuel!” And Eli understood that the Lord was calling the boy. Therefore Eli said to Samuel, “Go and lie down, and if he calls you, you shall say, ‘Speak, Lord, for your servant hears.’”

translator_notes.note: The narrator notes that “Samuel did not yet know the Lord”—a statement about experiential encounter, not theological ignorance. The passage suggests that recognizing God’s voice requires both personal readiness and the guidance of a spiritual mentor (Eli). This became a key text for spiritual direction traditions.
